speaker: interviewer
text: Interview with Mr. Kurt Lewin. How long did you hide in your apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until-- I was hiding in the apartment for a few days, but my suggestion is-- this, needless to say, is a central event of my experiences under the Germans which, in a way, has dictated a variety of things at a later date. I have written a book, [NON-ENGLISH], which was published in Eretz, Israel, or Palestine, in 1947 or '46 by [NON-ENGLISH], describing in great detail this particular incident. I also have written an article, which was published in the Ukraine in [NON-ENGLISH] Academy of Science. And then, recently, I published a book, published in the United States by Fithian Press, and I described this particular instance in great detail. Therefore, there is probably no need to dwell on it more.

speaker: interviewer
text: You remained hidden, however.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was hiding while these people were running around loose in the streets catching Jews. And once this subsided-- I don't look particularly Jewish-- why, one day I decided, after two or three days, that's enough. So, I washed up, put on a suit, and went out. That's the end of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother and your two brothers, once your father was killed--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she wasn't aware of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother was not aware.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did she become aware of that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a lengthy process because, you see, my mother knew that my father went to [NON-ENGLISH] to the Metropolitan. He offered him shelter, and there was-- a rumor circulated that he was hidden by the Metropolitan, which I knew was not true because I've seen him shot. But I didn't go out of my way to tell it to my mother. The next day after this incident, two SS men came. They had a list, and asked for my father. So, my mother said he was taken away. So, they beat her up and left. And this was part and parcel of the [NON-ENGLISH] operation, namely, that a group of SS men was traveling to the occupied territories, and taking prominent people, according to the previously prepared lists, and executing them. On this day, they executed [NON-ENGLISH], a prominent Polish writer, Bartel, former prime minister of Poland, prominent lawyers, Poles, leaders of the community, professors of university. They just were aiming at decapitating a society. My father was on this list and, therefore, they came to pick him up. But he was already dead at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did your mother find out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, a few months later. She left the apartment, went to a district which was inhabited by non-Jews. We had friends there. The husband of this lady actually probably died in Brygidki, because he was a banker. And we were staying there for two, three days, until the situation quieted itself in the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go with her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. No, she left and I joined her a few hours later.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, she left with a two younger children, and you joined later.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. Was in a shock. She was beaten up, after all. My mother was, at that time, 33 years old, without any means, hunger, and three children.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you went out-- as you said, you walked out-- where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: I joined my mother in this district inhabited by non-Jews, on the other side of the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you subject to any more--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: By the Germans at all, or the Ukrainians?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because I don't look Jewish. Well, I didn't look Jewish at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have identification of any sort?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that was not necessary. I speak fluently Polish. I didn't speak Ukrainian, and I speak Polish without a trace of an accent.

speaker: interviewer
text: During this time, what was-- how were rules and pogroms and stuff going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were no rules.

speaker: interviewer
text: There were none.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were no rules.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know what to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I just kept my eyes open. Whenever I saw commotion, I tried to move the other way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there restrictions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not yet. Was there a ghetto yet?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, everything was left alone.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long was it left alone?

speaker: interviewee
text: The restrictions came very gradually. The first thing they announced, the Jews are not allowed to use city parks. Then they have closed a district, which was the most luxurious one, and have actually evacuated everybody, and this was designed for the German civilian population, which came to occupy administrative positions in the city, and for military. And then they have enlarged this district and told the Jews to get out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there many Jews living in that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, because we intermingled. So, the whole process, you see, of collecting Jews in the ghetto, was very gradual, and Jews never knew what the ghetto is.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did the people who were evacuated from these luxurious houses go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they went to their friends, and eventually the Judenrat was assigning apartments and dwellings to these people, and that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: When your mother left-- I know you said that she was in a state of shock-- and you left afterwards, what did you take with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your possessions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing, the apartment was intact. They didn't rob anything, didn't take anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever go back to that apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. We went back there. We lived there until Jews had to leave this particular district and move to a more restricted area, but that's much later.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, your first was not to go to parks, and then they were evacuating people. And how else did the restrictions manifest themselves?

speaker: interviewee
text: You could be caught on the street and taken to forced labor, and either you came back or you didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever caught on the street?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, many a time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of forced labor did your--

speaker: interviewee
text: The railroad station.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would you do at the railroad station?

speaker: interviewee
text: We just dragged rails and all kinds of things. Sometimes, if you're lucky, we worked for a German military units, and they made us work but they didn't beat us up. If you were unlucky, you were beaten, and that's the end of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you were taken for labor did you-- were you released in the evening? Or was it just for a day, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Just for a day. Usually what happened is, they had trucks standing on the street, and they were catching men-- at that time only men-- taking them where it was need to do some work, whatever the work is. Either to remove rubble from bombing, or to repair roads, or to work at the railroad station, which was very heavily bombed, and things of that sort.

speaker: interviewer
text: How often did you get caught to do this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was caught three or four times. And then I didn't like the idea, so I went out every morning at 4 o'clock, and I worked in outside of the city, far in the suburbs. This was under the Soviets. They had there sort of a cooperative producing vegetables, and I used to go there because there it was quiet and we were not beaten. We volunteered there, and as I liked agriculture and things of that sort, I enjoyed working there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is this, at this point, under the Germans or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, they were locals. But the locals had free labor. We used to bring our own food, and that's it. So it was a reasonably decent way to spend the day and go home at night.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was a Judenrat set up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Immediately.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was in it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there were several Judenrats, and that is a complicated question. And I think, again, this was very carefully described and studied, so I'd rather not go into details of the Judenrat mechanism. Originally, these were leaders of the community, and they tried to protect the community. About four weeks after the Germans arrived, the general in charge of the city-- infantry general, not an SS-- imposed an enormous ransom on the Jewish community, and silver and all the precious things, jewelry, had to be brought to the Judenrat. Otherwise, reprisals were threatened.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, this-- carpets, people brought it simply to buy peace, and this was transported, probably, to Germany. This Judenrat was still organized and sensible group of people, headed by [NON-ENGLISH], Yousef [NON-ENGLISH] When they came to him and said, you have now to give us a few hundred people for labor camps, he refused. He said, money, yes, cooperation, yes, human beings, no. So, they took him to a prison and shot him with the rest of the Judenrat. That's the end of the story, and then a new Judenrat showed up. Who were the leaders-- who were picked to be Judenrat leaders?

speaker: interviewee
text: There's no criteria--

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't mean names, necessarily.

speaker: interviewee
text: I cannot answer that. I really couldn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: You did not know that. Did you have any dealings with the Judenrat yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in the [NON-ENGLISH] Yousef [NON-ENGLISH] Judenrat. My mother got me there, because while you were in the Judenrat you had a little piece of paper, so you were left alone. And I was a clerk in the statistical department, where they were preparing lists. This was, in my opinion, already set up to have better control of the Jewish population, and set up the mechanism for its destruction, eventual destruction.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of lists were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: Names and addresses.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anything more than names and addresses?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Address of a house on a street, and who the Jews are who dwell there. End of story.

speaker: interviewer
text: The second Judenrat, the one who followed [NON-ENGLISH]--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was not there, because I was taken with other clerks to the same prison, and I escaped from there. But that's, again, a long story. It's described in my book.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you taken, which prison?

speaker: interviewee
text: The name of the person was [NON-ENGLISH]. And whoever entered [NON-ENGLISH] rarely came out alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you captured to go there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they came with trucks, loaded us on trucks, brought us to the prison.

speaker: interviewer
text: From where did they find--

speaker: interviewee
text: At the Judenrat.

speaker: interviewer
text: At the Judenrat.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there was several buildings because the whole administration had to be organized. It was what-- [NON-ENGLISH], labor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it? Lung?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] means a labor department.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, but-- the name of the prison.

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] It's a Polish name. [NON-ENGLISH]. It's an obscure name.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: About two, three days, and we were taken out to be shot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this part of an [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get out of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I jumped out of the truck.

speaker: interviewer
text: Alone? Anybody else jump--

speaker: interviewee
text: There were some others.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you hurt?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was not. Some were hurt, were shot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were shot.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Exactly where did you go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took people to Piaski, which was a very notorious place of execution by the Germans at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Piaski.

speaker: interviewee
text: Piaski. Suburb-- it was essentially a sand quarry.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, you didn't get to Piaski. You escaped before that.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Home. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You went home.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And home was your old apartment.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, yes, yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they come looking for you again?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. Once you got out, you got out.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: After that, I was still hanging around the city for a while and observing the situation. I came to conclusion that I have to get out, and I want to Rawa Ruska.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was in Rawa Ruska? Your grandparents were still there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Rawa Ruska was a strange city. It was a sea of the local administration of the districts, which means the court, schools, police, under the Poles. And the Jewish community, primarily Jew-- maybe 80% of the population was Jewish. It was a service town. It's livelihood was strange, because they were importing scraps of furs from Leipzig, which was a very important fur center. And it was fashionable in central Europe, and to some extent western Europe, too, for women to wear the lamb's coat, lamb's fur coat.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, the scraps as you cut the skins were packed into huge sacks, were sent to this town, and there the Jews were sitting and sewing it together into units which were shipped back to Leipzig. That is the way they were eking out a living. By the way, how far is Rawa Ruska from Lwow?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 50 miles.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do in Rawa Ruska?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went there with a cousin of my mother, and we were there-- we got ourselves a job as collectors of rags, scrap metal, and it was a subsidiary of Goering's workers. Hermann Goering created an industrial empire for himself in Germany, and Austria, and Czechoslovakia and, in addition to that, there were subsidiaries. And one of these civilian Germans was running a subsidiary, which was collecting rags, glass, scrap iron.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live there?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Rawa Ruska?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: I lived in the office, which means there was a little office, and there I had my little corner, and at night I had two blankets, and that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: The office was in the German--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Rawa Ruska. No, just in the city, center of the city. A tiny little office.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were under German domination.

speaker: interviewee
text: Obviously, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you being treated there, working?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we worked there-- there there were no Germans in this whole-- it was run by Jews and some Poles, so it was no problem. You were not paid anything, but we had what was called good papers, which allowed us not to be caught. What you refer to as [NON-ENGLISH], Because it was vulgarly called as [NON-ENGLISH]. [GERMAN] in German means useful, [GERMAN], Jew-- a useful Jew. And even there was an aluminum plaque to that effect.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have identification--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, I have it here.

speaker: interviewer
text: --plaque of identification.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I don't have the placard.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any outward, outside symbols of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Every Jew had to wear, under penalty of death, an arm band with the Star of David.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many hours did you work?

speaker: interviewee
text: As much as I wanted to. It was not really the work-- we paid the Germans and the Poles for the papers, and we probably just were delivering symbolically some scraps here to elsewhere, which were collected by Jews whose livelihood this was before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about food, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's very hard to answer. You had to just simply, in a way, take care of yourself. The point is, that while in Lwow there was continuous pressure, continuous violence, and presence of Germans, and constant danger. In Rawa Ruska, they paid off the five or six German policemen, and the Ukrainian police was controlled by the Germans, who kept them in check. So, there was no violence when I arrived there for a few months. Then the whole thing started there, too, but at a much lower pace.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have contact with your mother during this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the time, because the post was functioning normally, and I got myself permits, which was rather unusual, to travel by railroad. So, I used to go to Lwow every two, three months to visit my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you would go, you would wear all your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, yes. It was very dangerous, because I could be suddenly caught and people, say, take my documents away, tear them, and shoot me. So, it was taking my life into my hands each and every time I went on the railroad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a ghetto in Rawa Ruska?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but it was not closed yet. Slowly, it was the same process. Namely, removal of Jews from the luxurious areas. Then, squeezing them into smaller, and smaller area. But the ghetto was not yet defined or closed.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had mentioned that you were in the Lwow ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before my escape.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before your escape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to move out of your house, or did the ghetto move into your area?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my mother moved first to one place, and then to the final ghetto. She moved twice.

speaker: interviewer
text: She moved twice.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you move with her?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in Rawa Ruska.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, you never moved to a ghetto with your mother, but you visited her there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Regularly, stayed there a few days, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did she move to? Where did you visit her?

speaker: interviewee
text: She lived-- the first move was to what used to be a Jewish quarter. It was a [NON-ENGLISH]. Actually, if you are interested, this was a square. They used to sell fish in huge wooden tubs. It was not too far from the orthodox synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she take stuff from her house? Did you help her move, or did she do it on her own?

speaker: interviewee
text: She has done it on her own, and she moved only few possessions because the furniture, everything else, was left behind. It was impossible to take with the limitation. The second move was into the final ghetto. I escaped shortly before it was closed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, you lived in that second place.

speaker: interviewee
text: For two weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you help her move there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, I wasn't there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How large was that second-- second ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: The second place was one apartment shared by three families. So, first she lived in an apartment by herself with the children. Then she shared an apartment with three more families. And then finally, everybody had three square meters assigned.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you with her then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were the people that were around you, that you were sharing space with? Did you know them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. In their first apartment, it was the widow of my uncle, who was shot with my father, also a prominent rabbi, his daughter, who was a child, and some other people whom I don't any longer remember. The final was the director of the gymnasium which I attended, and his wife, and many other people I don't really recall.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did people get along?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very well. Much better than people here in New York, despite all these extraordinary pressures. Furthermore, they were clean. They didn't run that around in jeans and look like refugees, like people in New York. They, under the circumstances, still maintained human dignity.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any social relationships? Did people get married in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you attend any--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't attend, but I'm aware. But why did people get married? A sister of my friend, a very beautiful girl, much older-- slightly older than I, two or three years, married a Jewish policeman because, by marrying him, she was taken out of the [NON-ENGLISH], probably for three or four weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about education? Were there any kind of programs for kids?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no. Forget about it. Not in Lwow, because--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about death? When somebody died, what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: They buried him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did they--

speaker: interviewee
text: The cemetery.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the cemetery within the confines of the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, they had Jewish cemeteries.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there babies born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember any kind of ceremonies around that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. You see, you ask questions about expressions of normal community life. These are abnormal circumstances, and all I can tell you is, in order to put it in a proper perspective, try to imagine a pressure cooker with heat constantly increased, and people lived, literally, by the hour.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned the Jewish police.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they behave?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is a very complicated and a very delicate question. Why? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In the Jewish police and its command, there were some very decent people who tried their best to help, and alleviate the suffering. But, don't forget that they were created as a mechanism to keep order in the community, keep it quiet and docile. Eventually, their job was to identify where Jews live, and make it possible for the Germans to collect them in places where they were sent to the death camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, whether they were aware of it or not, very hard to say. A minority was quite beastly, robbed people. Others tried to help. Your own experience with any Jewish police?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I had no experience because, you see, I moved my own way. There were also those who where [NON-ENGLISH] men of the Gestapo. Men who worked for the Gestapo, who were agents of the Gestapo.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, surely, and one of them, a prominent Jewish real estate operator here in New York, Bruce [NON-ENGLISH], was one of them. What his role was I don't know, but I know that he was one of the movers and shakers in the Jewish community in the last Judenrats, which were essentially a mechanism to collect and destroy the community.

speaker: interviewer
text: By the way, was there a wall around the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in the beginning. Eventually, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you there when the wall was erected?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was there when the wall was erected, but I wasn't there when they closed because I didn't like to wait for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of wall was it? What was it made of?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they used, usually, the area. For example, there was a railroad track which separated the ghetto from the rest of the city, and the rest was fenced in. Wooden planks--

speaker: interviewer
text: Wooden planks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Wire.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a guard at--

speaker: interviewee
text: Barbed wire. Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a guard at the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Always.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there more than one entrance?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were one or two entrances, simply because people used to march to work in the morning and come back in the evening.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who were the guards? Jewish police, or others?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish police, sometimes Ukrainian police. So, when you speak about the Jewish police, you see, it's very hard to say that they were all collaborators, and murderous, and henchmen. Not necessarily. It's very fairly complicated subject.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to change our tape again.

speaker: interviewee
text: Surely.
